transcription:
CSE 138 Lecture #5
- When using a logical clock, you must choose
the important elements to track
  - Only deliver a message when every
message in its causal history has been delivered
  - Overkill for implementing FIFO delivery
causal broadcast protocol
  - NOT totally ordered, as concurrent
messages can go in any order
[1,0,0,0]                    [0,0,0,1]
        VCs of independent events
      neither is bigger
  - They could still disagree, which would be
problematic

- Totally ordered + causally ordered message
delivery is possible, but hard (w/ fault tolerance)

Unicast Message: One sender, one receiver

Broadcast Message: One sender, everyone receives
                            (including sender)
Multicast Message: One sender, multiple receivers
                        (not necessarily everybody)

Distributed Snapshot
  - We need an algorithm, a time of day
clock is not enough, no perfectly synchronized
-snapshot is of the state, which is just a
history of all events

-A snapshot is correct when it observes
it has all such that if an event is in the
snapshot, then all events that happen before
it should be too.

A snapshot satisfying this property is called
a consistent global snapshot

Chandy-Lamport algorithm

They could still disagree, which would be problematic
Totally ordered + causally ordered message delivery is possible, but hard (w/ fault tolerance)
